       lineItem        : [ { position: 40, label: 'Loyalty Points' } ],
       selectionField : [ { position: 40 } ]
    }
  Loyaltypoints;

  @ui.hidden: true
  Createdby;

  @ui.hidden: true
  Createdat;

  @ui.hidden: true
  Lastchangedby;

  @ui.hidden: true
  Locallastchangedat;

  @ui.hidden: true
  Lastchangedat;

}


Metadata extension for zlm_c_transctns_ee.

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
@UI: {
  headerInfo: { typeName: 'Loyalty Membership Transaction',
                typeNamePlural: 'Loyalty Membership Transactions',
                title: { type: #STANDARD, label: 'Loyalty Membership Transactions', value: 'Transactiondate' } } }
annotate entity zlm_c_transctns_ee with
{
  @ui.facet: [
     {
      id: 'Transactions',
      purpose: #STANDARD,
      label: 'Transactions',
      type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE
     }
   ]

  @ui.hidden: true
  Transactionuuid;
  @ui.hidden: true
  Membershipuuid;
  @ui.identification: [ {
    position: 10 ,
    label: 'Transaction date'
  } ]
  @ui.lineItem: [ {
    position: 10 ,
    label: 'Transaction date'
  } ]
  @ui.selectionField: [ {
    position: 10
  } ]
  Transactiondate;
  @ui.identification: [ {
      position: 20 ,
      label: 'Transaction value'
    } ]
  @ui.lineItem: [ {
    position: 20 ,
    label: 'Transaction value'
  } ]
  @ui.selectionField: [ {
    position: 20
  } ]
  Transactionvalue;
  @ui.identification: [ {
    position: 30 ,
    label: 'Loyalty points'
  } ]
  @ui.lineItem: [ {
    position: 30 ,
    label: 'Loyalty points'
  } ]
  @ui.selectionField: [ {
    position: 30
  } ]
  Loyaltypoints;
  @ui.hidden: true
  Createdby;
  @ui.hidden: true
  Createdat;
  @ui.hidden: true
  Lastchangedby;
  @ui.hidden: true
  Locallastchangedat;
  @ui.hidden: true
  Lastchangedat;
}

Service Definition & Binding

Create OData V4 service:
- Expose projection views
- Create service binding
- Publish service

This enables UI consumption.

Create service definition and service binding.

@EndUserText.label: 'OData V4 UI loyalty membership'
define service ZLY_UI_MEMBERSHIP_EE_O4 {
  expose zlm_c_membship_ee  as LoyaltyMembership;
  expose zlm_c_transctns_ee as MembershipTransactions;
}

 

Testing

  1. Open binding
  2. Launch Fiori app
  3. Create membership
  4.  Add transaction
  5.  Verify loyalty points calculation
  6. Check that data is saved in external HANA database.

End-to-End Flow

  1. User enters data in UI
  2. RAP behavior triggers
  3. Data stored temporarily in buffer
  4. Save method writes to external DB
  5. Commit completes transaction
  6. UI displays updated data

Key Takeaways.

  1. RAP model separates data, behavior, and UI
  2. Logical schema handles secure connectivity
  3. Business logic is implemented in ABAP classes
  4. No data replication required

Further info

  • External Entities | SAP Help Portal
  • External Entities (ABAP Keyword Documentation)
  • Working with Logical External Schemas | SAP Help Portal
  • Working with Outbound Services | SAP Help Portal
  • Communication Management | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Start remote Process/Actions in BTP ABAP via Task Chains from SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Modern cloud architectures allow for versatile and open interoperability. So does SAP Datasphere and SAP BTP ABAP, too. This example outlines some essentials how Datasphere can kick-off actions defined on the ABAP Cloud side, beyond system boundaries.

Starting a Task/Process in BTP ABAP remotely from SAP Datasphere using Task Chains opens up new scenarios for interoperability and integration. The goal is to orchestrate remote tasks initiated by SAP Datasphere — securely, programmatically, and fully automated.

We will cover the below points:

  • Creating a Task Chain in Datasphere including an API Call to ABAP Cloud
  • Connection in Datasphere to ABAP Cloud (for direct API call)
  • Setting up Communication System & Arrangement in ABAP Cloud
  • Executing the Task Chain in Datasphere: Trigger actions in ABAP Cloud

 

1 Architecture Overview

Architecture

The SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Task: Datasphere acts as the caller. ABAP Cloud exposes an endpoint (inbound Web Api) to trigger an action.

Option (2) and (3) complete the picture of possible solution approaches. Main differences as well as pros & cons are outlined further down in one of the last sections.

The Solution Architecture displays 3 different options:

  • (1) Use Direct API Integration 
    • Straightforward
    • Minimal cost and complexity
    • Perfect for nightly batch orchestration, event-driven data loads, or simple API-driven analytical data refresh
  • (2) Integration Suite
    • Integration involves complex message transformations, protocol mediation, multiple target systems, or when centralized API governance and monitoring across the enterprise are required.
    • Especially appropriate where iFlow reusability justifies the additional cost and setup effort or when integration suite is already in place and a common tool.
  • (3) CAP Application
    • Custom application logic is needed beyond what a direct API call/iFlow can provide
    • E.g. multi-source data aggregation, advanced parsing, enrichments
    • Additional persistence required
    • Well-suited for reusable microservice-style integrations on BTP

 

2 Prerequisites

You need

  1. SAP Datasphere tenant with developer + admin access
  2. Task Chain to design the flow of events
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment (ABAP Cloud) with developer access
  4. BTP ABAP: Authorization for OAuth clients, Communication Arrangements, ABAP development of Web APIs

 

 3 Creating Communication Artifacts in ABAP Cloud

Navigation: ABAP Environment Web Access → Communication Management → Communication Systems/Arrangements

Communication Arrangement

 

Communication Arrangement

Communication System

Communication System 1

Communication/Connection User:

Create a User for Inbound Communication and use the same in the subsequent step on Datasphere level for the creation of a generic HTTP connection.

 

4 ABAP Cloud Artifacts

For the sake of simplicity I won't go into the details of all of the development artifacts and configuration settings.

For sure, you will create:

  • Inbound Service
  • Service Binding
  • Communication Scenario BTP ABAP
  • Service Definition
  • Entity
  • Class implementation

An example of an action definition processData (ZI_DEMO_SERVICE):

managed implementation in class zbp_i_demo_service unique;

define behavior for ZI_DEMO_SERVICE alias DemoService
persistent table zdemoservice
lock master
{
  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( numbering : managed ) Id;

  create;
  update;
  delete;

  action processData result [1] zdemoservice;

  mapping for zdemoservice
  {
    Id = id;
    Name = name;
    Description = description;
    Status = status;
    CreatedBy = created_by;
    CreatedAt = created_at;
    LastChangedBy = last_changed_by;
    LastChangedAt = last_changed_at;
    LocalLastChanged = local_last_changed;
  }
}

An example of an ABAP Class with an action implementation:

CLASS lhc_DemoService DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
 
    PRIVATE SECTION.
 
    METHODS processData FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION DemoService~processData result result.
 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_DemoService IMPLEMENTATION.
  
  METHOD processData.
    " Your business logic implementation her
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Service Definition:

@EndUserText.label: 'Demo Service Definition'
define service ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE {
  expose ZC_DEMO_SERVICE as DemoService;
}

Inbound Service:

Communication Scenario:

 

5 Create a Connection

The first step is creating a generic HTTP connection to the endpoint exposed by ABAP Cloud. In order to do so, consider the following details. Take the connection host from the BTP ABAP instance key. The credentials must be taken from the connection user as defined in the communication arrangement.

 

An alternative to Username/Password is an OAuth based authentication mechanism. 

 

6 Creating a Task Chain in SAP Datasphere

The first step is defining a Task Chain that encapsulates the processing logic you want to execute .

In the given example:

  • The Task Chain starts with a API Task
  • Sends an email in case of success

Task Chain

In the API Task details, define the following:

  • Method POST
  • API Path - the path to the service endpoint of your ABAP Web API that you want to trigger
  • Mode Synchronous

API Task Details 1

 

Make sure to fetch the CSRF Token. The URL which I've used is simply the $metadata path of the service endpoint:

https://<ABAP_HOST>.abap.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE_O4/$metadata

 

7 Execution Results

The Task Chain in Datasphere successfully executed the ABAP Web API call:

Task Chain API successfully executed

For monitoring and troubleshooting purpose, individual API calls can be traced from BTP ABAP side. The HTTP trace can be turned on for granular levels.

 

8 Solution Options - Comparisons

The following comparison outlines the key aspects per solution/architecture option as depicted in the initial architecture diagram. I have prototyped all of the below and all options are working flawlessly. Especially w.r.t. to the CAP based solution, there is a wide open room to play with.

 

Option

Pros (+)

Cons (-)

(1) Direct API

  • Simplest setup & fewest components & lowest cost
  • No middleware latency
  • Lightweight and developer-friendly
  • Limited transformation capability
  • Less suitable for multi-step or conditional routing logic

(2) Integration Suite

  • Supports complex transformations, multiple protocols and error handling in iFlows
  • Centralized API governance via API Management
  • Recommended for complex mediated flows
  • Two-step connectivity required (Integration Suite + Datasphere)
  • Additional licensing cost
  • Higher setup and maintenance effort

(3) CAP CF App

  • Maximum flexibility for custom logic (parsing, filtering, enrichment, retry)
  • CAP integrates natively with HANA Cloud/Datasphere HDI
  • Supports secure credential management
  • Reusable microservice pattern
  • Requires application development and BTP CF deployment skills
  • Higher operational overhead
  • More BTP resource consumption
  • CAP app lifecycle must be managed independently

 

9 Conclusion/Outlook & Further aspects:

Overview of the major building blocks:

Capability

Result

No hardcoded secrets

via Communication Arrangements/System

Remote orchestration

Creating an end-to-end orchestrated chain of tasks, bidirectional if required

Cloud-native ABAP

Using appropriate ABAP Class APIs

Datasphere automation

Using Task Chain API

 

Use Cases that the implementation enables:

  • Nightly batch orchestration
  • Event-driven data loads
  • Cross-system workflows (this was our ultimate focus)
  • API-driven (transactional/analytical) data refresh

Limitations:

  • The Task Chain/API Task can't be parameterized dynamically with runtime parameters to e.g. initiate calls that have a variable payload in header or body
  • Moreover, the write-back/storing of HTTP responses is limited too

References:

  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Documentation:  https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview
  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/c8a54ee704e94e15926551293243fd1d/274f2736465c4c48a091c67588...

This blog entry complements my other blog on Triggering SAP Datasphere/BDC Task Chains from SAP BTP ABAP Cloud using the Task Chain REST API.

In case of questions feel free to raise them and please leave a comment if you found something incorrect - thanks for any help on that. I'm hoping this blog can support somebody out there and may facilitate some problem solving.

entry.title: RAP Augmentation in Managed Scenario – Enriching Transactional Requests Before BO Processing
entry.content:
Introduction

The RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides several extension points for implementing business logic, such as Determinations, Validations, Actions, and Prechecks. RAP Augmentation adds another extension point that allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework starts processing the business object.

This early execution makes augmentation useful for scenarios where default values, audit information, or business-specific data must be available before any further RAP processing takes place. In this blog, we will understand RAP Augmentation, compare it with Determinations, and implement it using an Employee Management example.

Business Requirement

I had a requirement  an Employee Management application where users create and maintain employee records. During record creation, the application should automatically populate business-specific information instead of relying on users to enter every value manually.

The application should:

  • Set the employee status to PENDING_APPROVAL.
  • Determine whether approval is required based on the selected department.
  • Capture the current user as the creator.
  • Maintain audit information during updates.

Instead of implementing all this logic inside the business object, we will use RAP Augmentation to enrich the incoming request before standard processing begins.

Problem Statement

Determinations are commonly used in RAP to derive or update field values. However, they execute after the request has already entered the transactional buffer.

In some business scenarios, values must be available even before the business object starts processing. For example, default values or audit fields may be required before validations or other business logic execute. In such cases, Augmentation provides a cleaner and more suitable solution by enriching the request at the beginning of the transaction.

What is RAP Augmentation?

RAP Augmentation is an extension mechanism that runs during the Interactive Phase of a RAP transaction. It allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework processes the business object.

Rather than modifying persisted data, augmentation focuses on preparing the request by supplying additional values or applying business-specific logic. Once the request is enriched, RAP continues with its normal processing flow.

Augmentation Flow

The request passes through augmentation before reaching the business object implementation. During this stage, additional values can be added or modified, ensuring the business object receives a complete request for further processing.

The overall execution flow is:

  • User sends a create or update request.
  • RAP invokes the registered augmentation.
  • The request is enriched with additional data.
  • Standard RAP processing continues.
  • Data is persisted to the database.

Augmentation vs Determination

Defined at the Projection Behavior Definition using use create/update (augment).Defined at the Interface Behavior Definition using  determination.
Implemented in a Projection Behavior Implementation Class Implemented in the Behavior Pool  class.
Runs during the Interactive Phase, before the BO implementation processes the request.Runs during ON MODIFY or ON SAVE, after data enters the transactional buffer.
Used to enrich or modify the incoming request before BO processing.Used to derive or recalculate values based on data in the transactional buffer.

When Execution timing matter?

The main advantage of Augmentation is when it executes. Since it runs before business object processing, any values added during augmentation are available to the rest of the RAP processing flow.

  • If the application should automatically set an employee's status or determine whether approval is required, these values are already present when the framework performs subsequent processing. If the same logic were implemented in a Determination, the values would only be available later in the transaction.

When to Use Augmentation?

Augmentation is a good choice when the incoming request needs to be enriched before RAP starts processing the business object.

Typical use cases include:

  • Setting default values.
  • Populating audit fields.
  • Deriving values from user input.
  • Performing lightweight request enrichment.
  • Preparing data required by later processing steps.

It is not intended to replace Determinations, which remain the preferred option for business logic that should execute after the request has entered the transactional buffer.

 

BDEF of Interface

managed implementation in class zbp_aky_i_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_i_emp //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zaky_t_emp

lock master

authorization master ( instance )
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create( authorization : global ) ;
  update;
  delete;
  action Approve result [1] $self;
  mapping for zaky_t_emp
{
  Employeeid        = employeeid;
  Firstname         = firstname;
  Lastname          = lastname;
  Department        = department;
  Status            = status;
  Approvalrequired  = approvalrequired;
  Createdby         = createdby;
  Createdat         = createdat;
  Lastchangedby     = lastchangedby;
  Lastchangedat     = lastchangedat;
  }
}

BDEF of Projection

projection implementation in class zbp_aky_c_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_c_emp //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create( augment );
  use update( augment );
    use delete;
  field ( readonly ) Status;
  field ( readonly ) Approvalrequired;
  field ( readonly ) Createdby;
  field ( readonly ) Createdat;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedby;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedat;
  use action Approve;
}

BP of Projection

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS augment_create FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE zaky_c_emp.

    METHODS augment_update FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR UPDATE zaky_c_emp.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD augment_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    IF entity-Department IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = entity-%cid
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Department is mandatory' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_c_emp.        

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid )
      TO failed-zaky_c_emp.          
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp  
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      CREATE FIELDS ( Status Approvalrequired Createdby Createdat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %cid             = entity-%cid
        Status           = 'PENDING_APPROVAL'
        Approvalrequired = COND #(
                             WHEN entity-Department = 'HR'
                               OR entity-Department = 'FIN'
                             THEN abap_true
                             ELSE abap_false )
        Createdby        = sy-uname
        Createdat        = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD augment_update.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp   
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Lastchangedby Lastchangedat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        Employeeid    = entity-Employeeid
        Lastchangedby = sy-uname
        Lastchangedat = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

BP of interface

METHOD Approve.

  " Read current status of the employee
  READ ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zaky_i_emp
    FIELDS ( Status Employeeid )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(employees)
    FAILED DATA(read_failed).

  " Loop and update status
  LOOP AT employees INTO DATA(employee).

    " Only approve if current status is PENDING_APPROVAL
    IF employee-Status <> 'PENDING_APPROVAL'.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = employee-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Only records with PENDING_APPROVAL status can be approved' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_i_emp.
      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = employee-%tky )
      TO failed-zaky_i_emp.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    " Update status to APPROVED
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Status )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %tky   = employee-%tky
        Status = 'APPROVED'
      ) )
      FAILED   DATA(update_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(update_reported).

    " Pass back result
    APPEND VALUE #(
      %tky   = employee-%tky
      %param = employee
    ) TO result.

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Output

Click on the create 

After adding the value click on the create and put the debugger on the create-augment method to check it is triggering or not because we are doing managed application where creation is handled by framework

Execute

Currently it is in pending state click on the Approve employee.

After clicking on action

If you not adding the department

 

Conclusion

RAP Augmentation provides a simple and effective way to enrich transactional requests before the RAP framework begins processing the business object. It is particularly useful for populating default values, maintaining audit information, and preparing request data that should be available throughout the transaction.

In this example, we used augmentation to automatically populate employee details while keeping the business object implementation clean and maintainable. Understanding when to use Augmentation instead of Determinations helps developers choose the right extension point and build more maintainable RAP applications
